Achan took some of the spoil of Jericho. The love of the world is that root of bitterness, which is most difficult to remove. We should be careful about our own sin, so that many are not defiled or troubled; and we should avoid fellowship with sinners, so that we do not share their guilt. It is important for us to watch over one another to prevent sin, because others’ sins can harm us. The easy conquest of Jericho caused contempt for the enemy and a desire to expect the Lord to do everything without them using proper methods. Thus, people misuse the teachings about God's grace and the promises of God as excuses for their own laziness and indulgence. We are to work out our own salvation, even though it is God who works in us. It was a valuable victory for the Canaanites, by which Israel was awakened and reformed, reconciled to their God, and the people of Canaan were hardened toward their destruction.

Joshua’s concern for God's honor, more than even for the fate of Israel, expressed the language of the Spirit of adoption. He pleaded with God. He mourned their defeat, as he feared it would reflect on God’s wisdom and power, his goodness and faithfulness. We cannot make a better plea at any time than this: Lord, what will you do for your great name? Let God be glorified in all things, and then welcome his will.

God awakened Joshua to inquire by telling him that when this accursed thing was removed, everything would be well. Times of danger and trouble should be times of reformation. We should examine ourselves, our own hearts, and our own homes, and make a careful search to find if there is some accursed thing there that God sees and abhors; some secret desire, some unlawful gain, some withholding from God or from others. We cannot prosper until the accursed thing is destroyed from our hearts, removed from our homes and families, and forsaken in our lives. When the sin of sinners finds them out, God should be acknowledged. The righteous God does and will distinguish between the innocent and the guilty; so that even though the righteous are of the same tribe, family, and household as the wicked, they will never be treated as the wicked.

See the folly of those who promise themselves secrecy in sin. The righteous God has many ways of bringing to light hidden works of darkness. See also how much it is our concern when God is contending with us, to find out the cause that troubles us. We must pray with holy Job: Lord, show me why you contend with me. Achan’s sin began in his eyes. He saw these fine things, as Eve saw the forbidden fruit. See what comes of allowing your heart to follow your eyes, and how much we need to make a covenant with our eyes so that if they wander, they will surely weep for it. It proceeded out of the heart. Those who want to be kept from sinful actions must mortify and restrain sinful desires within themselves, especially the desire for worldly wealth. If Achan had looked upon these things with an eye of faith, he would have seen they were accursed things and would have dreaded them; but looking on them with an eye of sense only, he saw them as desirable things and coveted them. When he committed the sin, he tried to hide it. As soon as he got this plunder, it became his burden, and he dared not use his ill-gotten treasure. So differently do objects of temptation appear at a distance from what they do when they have been obtained. See the deceitfulness of sin; that which is pleasing in commission is bitter in reflection. See how those who rob God will be deceived. Sin is a very troublesome thing, not only to the sinner himself but to all around him. The righteous God will certainly reward with tribulation those who trouble his people. Achan did not perish alone in his sin. They lose their own who grasp at more than their own. His sons and daughters were put to death with him. It is likely that they helped to hide the things; they must have known of them. What fatal consequences follow, even in this world, for the sinner himself and all belonging to him! One sinner destroys much good. What then will be the wrath to come? Let us flee from it to Christ Jesus as the sinner’s Friend. There are circumstances in Achan's confession, marking the progress of sin, from its first entrance into the heart to its being done, which may serve as the history of almost every offense against the law of God and the sacrifice of Jesus Christ.
